content.text: Conditioning is the mechanism that makes diffusion models useful: it transforms pure noise into an image that satisfies one or more external signals. A model without conditioning produces a random sample from the learned data distribution. With conditioning, it produces a sample that lies at the intersection of that distribution and all supplied constraints — a text description, an edge map, a reference pose, a style LoRA. Understanding how each conditioning channel works, and how they compose, gives you precise control over generation quality.

During training, a diffusion model learns to reverse the process of gradually adding Gaussian noise to training images. Formally, the model learns a score function — the gradient of the log probability of clean data given a noisy observation. Unconditional training gives you a model that can generate plausible images from any point in the learned distribution, but you cannot steer it.
Conditional training modifies the score function: instead of learning
is the conditioning signal. The model is now a function that, given noisy latent
, predicts the noise that was added — guided by condition
. Different conditioning modalities are injected at different architectural points inside the U-Net.

The primary conditioning channel in Stable Diffusion is a text prompt processed by a CLIP text encoder. The encoder converts a tokenized prompt (up to 77 tokens) into a sequence of 768-dimensional vectors (SD 1.x) or 1280-dimensional vectors (SDXL). These vectors are the conditioning context
The U-Net contains cross-attention layers at multiple spatial resolutions. In each layer, spatial features form the query
, and text embeddings form the key

attrs.latex: K = W_K c_{\text{text}},\; V = W_V c_{\text{text}}

content.text: . Each spatial position in the latent can attend to any token in the prompt. This is where LoRA adapters live: LoRA fine-tuning adds low-rank updates
to these projection matrices, shifting which visual concepts map to which text tokens.

SDXL conditions on two text encoders simultaneously: OpenCLIP ViT-bigG (1280-dim) and OpenCLIP ViT-L (768-dim). Their outputs are concatenated to form a 2048-dim pooled embedding used in the timestep conditioning, and a 77×2048 sequence used in cross-attention. SDXL also conditions on original image resolution and crop coordinates — discrete metadata fed through additional Fourier embeddings, improving generation at non-standard sizes.

Text conditioning alone is not strong enough to reliably steer generation at inference time. Classifier-Free Guidance (Ho & Salimans, 2022) amplifies the effect of the condition by running two forward passes per denoising step — one conditioned on the prompt and one on an empty (null) prompt — and extrapolating in the direction of the conditioned estimate:
, the formula reduces to plain conditioned sampling with no amplification. At
, the model extrapolates toward the conditioned estimate, producing higher prompt fidelity at the cost of sample diversity and, at very high values, visual artifacts.
The CFG doubled compute cost (two U-Net passes per step) drove development of distilled models like LCM and Turbo that achieve similar guidance effects in fewer steps with a single pass.
can be replaced with a negative prompt condition
The update now moves toward the positive prompt and away from the negative prompt simultaneously. Common negative prompts like
suppress entire regions of the data distribution. This is especially useful for anatomy artifacts (
) that SD 1.5 and SDXL still struggle with.

from diffusers import StableDiffusionPipeline
import torch

pipe = StableDiffusionPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "runwayml/stable-diffusion-v1-5",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
).to("cuda")

image = pipe(
    prompt="a photorealistic portrait of a scientist in a lab, natural lighting",
    negative_prompt="cartoon, anime, low quality, blurry, deformed, extra limbs",
    guidance_scale=7.5,
    num_inference_steps=30,
).images[0]
Structural Conditioning: ControlNet and Its Scale
ControlNet injects spatial conditioning — edge maps, depth maps, pose skeletons — as residuals at each encoder block of the U-Net. The contribution is scaled by a
This operates independently from CFG. The final noise prediction at each timestep is:

attrs.latex: \tilde{\epsilon}_t = \epsilon_\theta(z_t, c_{\text{neg}}) + w \cdot [\epsilon_\theta(z_t, c_{\text{pos}}, c_{\text{ctrl}}) - \epsilon_\theta(z_t, c_{\text{neg}}, c_{\text{ctrl}})]

content.text: Both the positive and negative U-Net passes include the ControlNet residuals (at their respective scales), so the structural conditioning is always active regardless of the CFG direction. In practice, setting
matches the training regime; values above 1.0 can over-constrain and suppress texture.

from diffusers import StableDiffusionControlNetPipeline, ControlNetModel
from PIL import Image
import torch

controlnet = ControlNetModel.from_pretrained(
    "lllyasviel/sd-controlnet-canny",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
)
pipe = StableDiffusionControlNetPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "runwayml/stable-diffusion-v1-5",
    controlnet=controlnet,
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
).to("cuda")

canny_image = Image.open("edge_map.png")

image = pipe(
    prompt="a futuristic cityscape at sunset",
    negative_prompt="blurry, low quality",
    image=canny_image,
    controlnet_conditioning_scale=0.8,   # structural weight
    guidance_scale=7.5,                  # text CFG weight
    num_inference_steps=30,
).images[0]
These two parameters control orthogonal conditioning channels and must be tuned independently:
(default 7.5): Controls how strongly the text prompt drives generation. Higher = more prompt-literal, less diverse, potentially over-saturated.
(default 1.0): Controls how much the structural map (edges, depth, pose) constrains layout. Higher = layout more rigid, may clash with text-driven details.
Conflict resolution: when text and structural conditioning disagree, lowering
gives text more creative latitude; lowering
lets the model blend both without hard constraints.

LoRA adapters differ fundamentally from text or ControlNet conditioning: they modify the model's weight matrices rather than injecting external signals at attention layers. A LoRA shifts the model's prior — which concepts and textures it considers plausible — before any external conditioning is applied.
Formally, for each adapted weight matrix

contribution at inference. Because this is a weight modification, it is active across all timesteps, all spatial positions, and all U-Net operations simultaneously — not at a single injection point like cross-attention or ControlNet residuals.
Most LoRAs are trained with an activation token — a keyword like

or the subject name — embedded in every training prompt. At inference, including this token in the positive prompt maximises the LoRA's effect. Omitting the token while using the LoRA still biases the model (weight shift is always active) but reduces how strongly the LoRA-specific features appear in cross-attention.

from diffusers import StableDiffusionPipeline
import torch

pipe = StableDiffusionPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "runwayml/stable-diffusion-v1-5",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
).to("cuda")

# Load a style LoRA trained with activation token "sks style"
pipe.load_lora_weights("path/to/watercolour_lora.safetensors")

# Activation token "sks style" triggers LoRA-specific attention patterns
image = pipe(
    prompt="sks style portrait of a woman in a garden",
    negative_prompt="low quality, blurry",
    cross_attention_kwargs={"scale": 0.9},  # lora_scale
    guidance_scale=7.5,
    num_inference_steps=30,
).images[0]

All three conditioning channels — text (via CFG), structural (via ControlNet), and style (via LoRA) — act simultaneously during each denoising step. They are not mutually exclusive and do not need to be separately balanced against each other in a single formula; each operates at a different layer of the architecture.

attrs.alt: Conditioning diagram: text/structural/LoRA inputs, U-Net cross-attention, CFG formula, multi-condition combination

content.text: The order of composition can be described as:
LoRA modifies the U-Net weights once before generation begins (or at every step if fused). The model's prior is shifted toward the LoRA distribution.
At each denoising step, ControlNet processes the structural image through its trainable encoder copy, then adds zero-conv outputs to the U-Net's encoder hidden states.
Cross-attention layers inject text embeddings into the spatially-aware hidden states. LoRA-adapted
project both spatial and text features through the shifted weight manifold.
CFG extrapolates the noise estimate away from the unconditioned baseline (or negative prompt), amplifying all active conditions proportionally to
Multiple ControlNets can be active simultaneously. Their residuals are simply summed before being added to the U-Net:

from diffusers import StableDiffusionControlNetPipeline, ControlNetModel
import torch

canny_net = ControlNetModel.from_pretrained(
    "lllyasviel/sd-controlnet-canny",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
)
depth_net = ControlNetModel.from_pretrained(
    "lllyasviel/sd-controlnet-depth",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
)

pipe = StableDiffusionControlNetPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "runwayml/stable-diffusion-v1-5",
    controlnet=[canny_net, depth_net],  # list of ControlNets
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
).to("cuda")

image = pipe(
    prompt="an architect's blueprint rendered as a photorealistic building",
    image=[canny_image, depth_image],
    controlnet_conditioning_scale=[0.9, 0.6],  # per-ControlNet scales
    guidance_scale=7.5,
    num_inference_steps=30,
).images[0]
Beyond text and structural signals, diffusion models are conditioned on the current timestep

. This is embedded as a sinusoidal positional encoding and injected into each residual block via additive shift-and-scale (adaptive group normalization). The timestep signal lets the model know how much noise is present in
, calibrating its denoising strength. At high
(heavy noise), the model focuses on global structure; at low
(light noise), it refines fine texture details.
works: starting denoising at a late timestep
skips the global-structure-defining early steps and only runs the detail-refinement portion, preserving the original image's composition.
Denoising granularity; coarse vs detail balance
Conditioning at Inference: A Complete Example

The following example uses all three channels together: a style LoRA for watercolour aesthetics, a Canny ControlNet for structural constraint, and a CFG-amplified text prompt for subject and lighting description.

import torch
from diffusers import StableDiffusionControlNetPipeline, ControlNetModel
from PIL import Image
import cv2
import numpy as np

# 1. Load base model + ControlNet
controlnet = ControlNetModel.from_pretrained(
    "lllyasviel/sd-controlnet-canny", torch_dtype=torch.float16
)
pipe = StableDiffusionControlNetPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "runwayml/stable-diffusion-v1-5",
    controlnet=controlnet,
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    safety_checker=None,
).to("cuda")

# 2. Load style LoRA (weight-space conditioning)
pipe.load_lora_weights("watercolour_style.safetensors")

# 3. Extract structural condition from a reference photo
photo = np.array(Image.open("reference.jpg").convert("RGB"))
edges = cv2.Canny(photo, threshold1=100, threshold2=200)
canny_image = Image.fromarray(np.stack([edges] * 3, axis=-1))

# 4. Run with all three conditions active
image = pipe(
    # Text conditioning (CFG = 8.0 amplifies this)
    prompt="sks watercolour painting of an old harbour at sunset, loose brushwork",
    negative_prompt="photorealistic, digital art, hard edges, low quality",
    guidance_scale=8.0,                         # CFG scale w

    # Structural conditioning
    image=canny_image,
    controlnet_conditioning_scale=0.75,         # ControlNet scale s

    # LoRA scale (weight-space)
    cross_attention_kwargs={"scale": 0.85},     # lora_scale

    num_inference_steps=30,
    generator=torch.Generator("cuda").manual_seed(42),
).images[0]

image.save("conditioned_output.png")

Tuning guidance_scale and conditioning_scale is iterative. A practical starting point: fix guidance_scale=7.5, tune conditioning_scale until layout feels right (usually 0.7–1.0), then adjust lora_scale (0.7–1.0) for style intensity. Only change guidance_scale if the prompt feels underweighted or the image is over-saturated.
Some inference libraries expose per-token attention weights, allowing you to amplify or suppress individual words in the prompt. Compel (used by Diffusers) uses
syntax to scale attention scores for individual tokens by factors of 1.1 per

from compel import Compel

compel = Compel(tokenizer=pipe.tokenizer, text_encoder=pipe.text_encoder)

# "detailed" and "sharp" get 1.21× attention weight (1.1^2)
# "soft" gets 0.826× weight (1.1^-2)
prompt_embeds = compel(
    "portrait of a woman, (detailed)++ face, (sharp)++ eyes, (soft)-- background"
)

image = pipe(
    prompt_embeds=prompt_embeds,
    guidance_scale=7.5,
    num_inference_steps=30,
).images[0]

This technique modifies the cross-attention keys and values at the token level, giving finer control over which concepts dominate at each spatial location — a complement to the global amplification provided by CFG.

Conditioning in diffusion models is multi-channel: text (cross-attention + CFG), structural (ControlNet residuals), and style (LoRA weight deltas) operate at different U-Net injection points simultaneously.

CFG doubles inference cost (two forward passes per step) but is the primary amplifier for prompt fidelity. Typical range is 7–9 for SD 1.5; SDXL and distilled models often work well at lower values (4–6).
Negative prompts replace the unconditional baseline in the CFG formula — the denoising direction moves toward the positive prompt and away from the negative prompt at every step.
ControlNet conditioning_scale and LoRA lora_scale are independent axes. Tune them separately after fixing guidance_scale.
Timestep conditioning is always active and determines which aspects of the image are refined at each denoising step — exploited by img2img strength to skip early structure-defining steps.
